frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment:
Ananya: Main kisi par force karne wali baat nahi kar rahi, but shaadi ke baad thoda sindoor ya mangalsutra pehenne se ek settled, suhagan wali grace aati hai. Aajkal ki ladkiyan ise regressive bol deti hain, par hamari parampara ka bhi toh ek mahatva hai.
Husband: Haan, choice bhi hai aur sanskriti bhi.
Ananya: Exactly, choice hi hai, lekin bilkul kuch na pehno toh ajeeb lagta hai, jaise shaadi ko importance hi nahi diya.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covert misogyny because it frames visible marriage markers as cultural grace and a natural sign of being a proper married woman, while judging women who do not wear them. It encodes tradition framing, completeness/settledness framing, and non-wearing judgment masked as 'personal choice'.
persona_text: You are Ananya. Age 34. A woman working as a deputy manager at a government bank in Lucknow. You are talking to your husband at home after coming back from a particularly difficult day. Your male junior colleague who you trained for two years just got promoted above you. Your branch manager told you informally that you are doing great but that the customers in your area respond better to male relationship managers for high value accounts. You are telling your husband what was said.
